rows.v: A neutral third party that holds money and documents during a transaction, releasing them only when all conditions are met. Protects both buyer and seller.
Their deposit sat safely in escrow until the inspection passed and the deal officially closed.
From Old French 'escroue' (a scroll or roll of writing) — originally a deed held by a third party until conditions were fulfilled.
A deposit a buyer puts down to show they're serious about an offer. It's credited toward the purchase at closing, but can be forfeited if the buyer backs out improperly.
She included $5,000 in earnest money with her offer to prove to the seller she was committed.
From 'earnest' in its old sense of a pledge or down payment given to seal a bargain — a token of serious intent dating back centuries.
A condition that must be met for a sale to proceed — like financing, inspection, or appraisal. If a contingency fails, the buyer can usually walk away without penalty.
His offer included a financing contingency, so when the loan fell through, he got his deposit back.
From Latin 'contingere' (to touch, befall) — the deal is contingent on these conditions being satisfied. The buyer's main escape hatches.
A professional, independent estimate of a property's market value, usually required by lenders to ensure they aren't loaning more than the home is worth.
The deal hit a snag when the appraisal came in $20,000 below the agreed price, spooking the lender.
From 'appraise' (to set a price on), via Latin 'pretium' (price). The appraisal protects the lender from over-lending on an inflated price.
A detailed examination of a property's condition by a professional, checking structure, systems, and safety before a buyer commits.
The inspection revealed a cracked foundation, giving the buyers leverage to renegotiate the price.
From Latin 'inspicere' (to look into). A buyer's key tool for discovering problems the seller may not have disclosed.
The fees and expenses, beyond the purchase price, paid when a real estate deal is finalized — lender fees, title insurance, taxes, and more.
Beyond the down payment, they had to budget another 3% in closing costs to seal the deal.
Named for the 'closing' — the final meeting where ownership transfers. These bundled fees often surprise first-time buyers.
The careful investigation a buyer does before committing — reviewing documents, inspections, finances, and risks. Doing your homework before you sign.
During due diligence, they uncovered an unpermitted addition that changed their whole view of the property.
A legal and finance term meaning the 'diligence' (care) that is 'due' (owed) before a deal — adopted widely into real estate practice.
The status of a property once a seller has accepted an offer but before the sale closes. The deal is agreed but not yet final, pending contingencies.
The house went under contract within a weekend, but it stayed that way for a month until closing.
Descriptive: the parties are now bound by a signed contract, though the transaction isn't complete until all conditions clear.
The portion of a home's price the buyer pays upfront in cash, with the rest financed by a mortgage. A larger down payment means a smaller loan.
They saved for years to afford a 20% down payment and avoid extra insurance costs.
A plain compound — the payment made 'down' front before financing the balance. The 20% threshold is a long-standing lending benchmark.
A lender's conditional commitment to loan a buyer up to a certain amount, based on a review of their finances. It makes offers far more credible to sellers.
With a pre-approval letter in hand, their offer stood out against buyers who hadn't secured financing.
Descriptive: the loan is 'approved' in advance ('pre-'), subject to final conditions — a stronger signal than a mere 'pre-qualification'.
Annual Percentage Rate — the yearly cost of a loan including interest plus fees, expressed as a percentage. A truer cost than the interest rate alone.
The two loans had the same interest rate, but the higher APR revealed one carried far steeper fees.
Mandated by lending-disclosure laws to let borrowers compare loans on equal footing, folding fees into a single annual figure.
Private Mortgage Insurance — extra insurance a borrower must pay when their down payment is under 20%, protecting the lender (not the buyer) if they default.
Because they put down only 10%, they owed monthly PMI until their equity reached the 20% mark.
An acronym for the insurance product; it exists to make low-down-payment loans less risky for lenders, at the borrower's expense.
The gradual paying off of a loan through regular payments over time. Early payments go mostly to interest; later ones go mostly to principal.
The amortization schedule showed that after ten years, she'd barely dented the principal of her 30-year loan.
From Latin 'mortus' (death) via Old French — the debt is gradually 'killed off' payment by payment until it reaches zero.
Two mortgage types: a 'fixed-rate' loan keeps the same interest rate for its whole term; an 'ARM' (adjustable-rate mortgage) starts lower but can change over time.
They chose a fixed-rate mortgage for certainty, wary that an ARM's payments could spike years later.
'Fixed' for its unchanging rate; 'ARM' an acronym capturing that the rate 'adjusts'. The choice is a classic bet on stability versus early savings.
Upfront fees paid to a lender to lower the loan's interest rate — 'buying down' the rate. One point typically costs 1% of the loan amount.
By paying two points at closing, they bought a lower rate that would save them money over the long haul.
From the sense of a 'point' as one percent. A tool for trading cash now against interest savings later, worthwhile only if you keep the loan long enough.
The portion of a home the owner truly owns — its market value minus what's still owed on the mortgage. It grows as the loan is paid down and the value rises.
After five years of payments and rising prices, they'd built enough equity to borrow against it for a renovation.
From Latin 'aequitas' (fairness) — your fair, owned share of the property, separate from the bank's stake in it.
Multiple Listing Service — the shared database real estate agents use to list properties for sale and find them for buyers. The industry's central marketplace.
The moment the home hit the MLS, a dozen agents alerted their clients and showings began.
An industry cooperative system dating to the late 1800s, built on agents agreeing to share listings — and commissions — with one another.
Short for 'comparable sales' — recently sold properties similar to one being valued, used to estimate a fair price. The backbone of pricing a home.
To set the list price, the agent pulled comps from three similar homes sold nearby in the past few months.
A clipping of 'comparables'. The logic is simple: a home is worth roughly what similar nearby homes recently sold for.
The price at which a property is advertised for sale. It's a starting point for negotiation, not necessarily the final sale price.
They set an aggressive list price, expecting a bidding war to push the final number even higher.
Named for the price on the 'listing'. Strategic pricing — sometimes deliberately low to spark competition — is an art in itself.
'DOM' — how long a property has been listed for sale. A high number can signal an overpriced home or hidden problems, weakening the seller's position.
With 90 days on market, the stale listing gave buyers confidence to come in well below asking.
Descriptive of the count of days a home sits 'on the market'. Buyers watch it closely as a clue to a seller's desperation.
Furnishing and decorating a home to make it more appealing to buyers — emphasizing space, light, and lifestyle. A marketing tool to sell faster and higher.
After professional staging, the empty house suddenly felt like a home, and offers came in within days.
From the theatrical 'staging' (setting a scene) — the home is dressed and arranged like a set to help buyers imagine living there.
How attractive a property looks from the street — the crucial first impression that draws buyers in before they ever step inside.
A fresh coat of paint and tidy landscaping boosted the curb appeal and doubled the weekend showings.
A vivid compound — the 'appeal' a home has when viewed from the 'curb'. First impressions, agents insist, are made before the front door opens.
A scheduled period when a listed home is open for any prospective buyer to tour without an appointment. A classic marketing event.
The Sunday open house drew thirty groups through the door and produced two competing offers.
Descriptive: the house is thrown 'open' to the public for viewing — a long-standing, low-pressure way to generate buyer interest.
A buyer's formal proposal to purchase at a stated price and terms; a 'counteroffer' is the seller's revised response. The back-and-forth of negotiation.
Their first offer was countered, and after two rounds of counteroffers they met in the middle.
From the plain sense of 'offering' to buy. The volley of offer and counteroffer is the heart of every real estate negotiation.
The legal right to own and use a property. A 'clear title' means no disputes or claims against it — essential for a clean sale.
A title search uncovered an old unpaid claim that had to be cleared before ownership could transfer.
From Latin 'titulus' (inscription, label) — the documented proof of who rightfully owns the property. Not to be confused with the deed.
The legal document that actually transfers ownership of property from one party to another. Signing and recording the deed makes the transfer official.
At closing, the seller signed the deed, and once recorded, the home legally belonged to the buyers.
From Old English 'dǣd' (something done) — the formal written act of transfer. The title is the right; the deed is the document conveying it.
A legal claim against a property for an unpaid debt, which can block a sale until it's resolved. Mortgages, taxes, and contractors can all place liens.
The sale stalled when a contractor's lien for unpaid work surfaced during the title search.
From Latin 'ligamen' (a binding, tie), via French 'lien' (bond) — the debt is legally 'tied' to the property until paid.
A legal right for someone to use part of another's property for a specific purpose — like a shared driveway or utility access — without owning it.
The deed noted an easement letting the neighbor cross the back corner to reach the lake.
From Old French 'aisement' (comfort, convenience) — a convenience granted over someone else's land. It 'runs with' the property to future owners.
Local government rules dictating how land in an area can be used — residential, commercial, industrial — and what can be built. Shapes what a property can become.
Their dream of opening a shop died when they learned the lot was zoned strictly residential.
From 'zone' (a designated area). Modern zoning emerged in the early 20th century to separate incompatible land uses and control growth.
Homeowners Association — an organization in many communities that sets rules and collects dues for shared upkeep and amenities. It can govern everything from paint colors to pets.
The HOA fees covered the pool and landscaping, but the strict rules on exterior changes gave them pause.
An acronym for the governing body of a planned community; its power to set rules and fees is a major consideration for many buyers.
A professional measurement of a property's exact boundaries, dimensions, and features. It settles disputes over where one lot ends and another begins.
The survey revealed the old fence actually sat two feet onto the neighbor's land.
From Old French 'surveeir' (to look over) — a precise mapping of the land's legal limits, often required before building or to resolve boundary questions.
'For Sale By Owner' (pronounced 'fizz-bo') — a home sold directly by the owner without a listing agent, to save the commission. Often harder than sellers expect.
The FSBO listing languished for months until the owner finally hired an agent to get it sold.
An acronym-turned-word from 'For Sale By Owner', pronounced 'FIZZ-bo' in the trade — agents' shorthand for an unrepresented seller.
A property in poor condition, priced low because it needs significant repairs or renovation. A bargain for those willing to do the work.
They bought a dated fixer-upper, planning to renovate the kitchen and baths themselves over a year.
A folksy compound — a home you must 'fix up'. A staple of the bargain-hunting and house-flipping world.
A property in move-in-ready condition, needing no work — you just 'turn the key' and live there. The opposite of a fixer-upper.
Busy professionals, they wanted a turnkey home and happily paid a premium to skip any renovations.
From the literal image of turning a key to move right in — nothing left to do. Borrowed from manufacturing, where a 'turnkey' system is ready to operate.
To buy a property, improve it quickly, and resell it for profit in a short time. A whole sub-industry of real estate investing.
He made a living flipping houses — buying distressed homes, renovating fast, and reselling within months.
From 'flip' in the sense of quickly turning something over. The speed of the buy-improve-sell cycle defines the strategy.
A listing-speak euphemism for an owner eager or pressured to sell quickly — often willing to accept a lower price. A signal of negotiating room.
The listing's 'motivated seller' line told her there was real room to negotiate below asking.
Agent code: 'motivated' politely signals the seller's urgency — from divorce, relocation, or financial pressure — without stating the reason.
An offer far below the asking price, hoping to snag a bargain. It can open negotiation or insult the seller, depending on the situation.
Their lowball offer of 30% under asking was rejected outright, and the seller refused to counter.
From 'low' plus 'ball' (as in throwing a pitch) — to 'lowball' is to pitch a deliberately low number, a tactic that can backfire.
